serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sweet paprika french fries is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a medium-sized portion of seasoned fried potato strips.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet Paprika French Fries — HPB Singapore Foodo Database (G00010371)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record G00010371 for Sweet Paprika French Fries. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB record; set to -1 where unavailable. Potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol are reported in the HPB source and have been carried forward.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for french fries / fried potato
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for sweet paprika french fries. GI of 75 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for deep-fried french fries (potato, deep-fried, GI range 63–85, central estimate ~75). Sweet paprika seasoning does not materially alter the glycaemic response of the potato base. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 39.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 44.3 g per 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 618 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit sweet paprika french fries due to sodium content of 618 mg/100 g; if consumed, request no added salt, avoid high-sodium dipping sauces, and restrict portion to no more than 50 g to keep sodium contribution below 310 mg per eating occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~75) combined with 39.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g drives rapid post-prandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat sweet paprika french fries as an occasional food only; if consumed, limit portion to 50–75 g, pair with a protein source and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and monitor blood glucose 1–2 hours post-meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying elevates total fat to 13.2 g/100 g and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); high sodium (618 mg/100 g) further burdens cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of deep-fried potato products such as sweet paprika french fries; the combination of high fat from frying, elevated sodium, and AGE formation from high-temperature cooking collectively increases atherogenic and hypertensive cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade ingredients (potato and sweet paprika) combined with deep-frying AGEs may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sweet paprika french fries; both potato and sweet paprika are nightshade-derived and may trigger symptom flares in sensitive individuals, while deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and the high sodium (618 mg/100 g) worsens tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet Paprika French Fries are deep-fried potato strips seasoned with sweet paprika, a mild, slightly sweet spice derived from dried and ground red bell peppers or similar Capsicum annuum varieties. The dish consists of potato batons cut to standard french-fry dimensions, deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crisp on the exterior with a soft, fluffy interior, then tossed or dusted with sweet paprika powder and salt. The result is a savoury, mildly smoky, and subtly sweet snack with a characteristic orange-red coating. In Singapore, this preparation appears on Middle Eastern restaurant menus and casual dining menus as a side dish or snack, often served alongside grilled meats or as a standalone appetiser. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 290 kcal, 3.31 g protein, 13.2 g fat, 39.5 g carbohydrate, 3.9 g dietary fibre, and 618 mg sodium. The high sodium content (618 mg/100 g) makes this food unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension. The high glycaemic index (~75) and substantial carbohydrate load make it a high-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes management. The deep-frying method elevates fat content and generates advanced glycation end-products, raising cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. Both potato and sweet paprika are nightshade vegetables, which may be relevant for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Potassium content is notable at 538 mg/100 g, providing some counterbalance to sodium, though insufficient to offset the hypertensive risk at typical serving sizes.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record G00010371 with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for deep-fried french fries; estimation basis clearly documented. Multilingual names derived from standard culinary and linguistic references for Singapore context.
notes: sugar_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record; this is treated as confirmed zero per the approved HPB source — sweet paprika contributes negligible sugar and the potato base after deep-frying has minimal free sugars. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo record G00010371 and not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific seasoned preparation as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB source record for this dish and no approved secondary source provides values for this specific preparation. food_group corrected from input value 'Meat and alternatives / Beef' to 'Snacks / Fried potato snacks' as sweet paprika french fries are a potato-based fried snack product, not a meat or beef product; the original classification appears to be a data entry error. The input health_suitability caution for rheumatoid arthritis references 'Trans fat 0.1g/100g' but trans_fat_g is not reported in the HPB source; this value has not been carried forward as it lacks approved-source support and is set to -1.
